title: Introduction
tagline: Master the core RL loop before formulas: who acts, what changes, what feedback means, and why long-term return matters.
masteryQuestion: Explain, without notes, how an agent uses state, action, reward, return, policy, value, and model to improve decisions over time.

vocabulary.definition: The learner and decision-maker that selects actions and improves from feedback.
vocabulary.intuition: Do not think of the agent as just a model. Think of it as the whole decision system: perception, memory, action-selection, and updating. In a real system, the agent is the part that can say, ‘Given what I know now, I will do this next.’
vocabulary.useCase: When designing an RL problem, first draw a boundary around what gets to decide. Everything inside that boundary is the agent; everything outside becomes environment.
vocabulary.tradingExample: A trader or bot deciding buy, sell, hold, hedge, or reduce size based on market state and past outcomes.
vocabulary.agentExample: An AI assistant deciding whether to search, ask clarification, call a tool, or answer directly.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like a role: the chooser inside the system boundary.

vocabulary.definition: Everything outside the agent that reacts to actions and returns new situations and feedback.
vocabulary.intuition: The environment is not just a physical place. It is the source of consequences. It contains the market, users, physics, game rules, APIs, delays, noise, and all the things the agent does not directly control.
vocabulary.useCase: If a behavior produces surprising consequences, you probably modeled the environment too narrowly. Expand the environment boundary until the feedback source is included.
vocabulary.tradingExample: The market, liquidity, volatility, news, fills, slippage, and other traders responding to orders.
vocabulary.agentExample: The user, files, APIs, browser pages, tool outputs, and errors that respond to an agent’s actions.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like pressure: the outside world that answers back.

vocabulary.definition: The information representation the agent uses to decide what to do now.
vocabulary.intuition: State is a compressed version of reality. A good state keeps the information that matters for the next decision and drops noise. A bad state hides critical context, making even a smart policy behave blindly.
vocabulary.useCase: Before choosing an algorithm, ask: what must be visible for a good decision? That answer is your state design problem.
vocabulary.tradingExample: Ticker, trend, volatility, position, risk budget, macro regime, support/resistance, and time to expiration.
vocabulary.agentExample: Current task, user preference, conversation context, file tree, tool results, constraints, and unresolved questions.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like context: the snapshot used before choosing.

vocabulary.definition: A choice available to the agent in a given state.
vocabulary.intuition: Actions are the agent’s control surface. If important choices are missing from the action set, the agent cannot learn them no matter how good the reward is. RL quality depends heavily on giving the agent the right levers.
vocabulary.useCase: List actions as verbs: wait, buy, sell, ask, search, summarize, retry, escalate. If it is not actionable, it is not an action.
vocabulary.tradingExample: Buy shares, sell calls, enter a debit spread, close a position, hold, or reduce size.
vocabulary.agentExample: Read a file, run tests, browse a site, create a PR, ask a question, or produce the final answer.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like a lever: the move the agent can actually take.

vocabulary.definition: The immediate scalar signal that says how good or bad the last transition was for the objective.
vocabulary.intuition: Reward is a teaching signal, not the whole goal. It is local feedback that nudges learning. If the reward is poorly designed, the agent can learn the wrong behavior very efficiently.
vocabulary.useCase: Reward design is objective design. Ask whether the signal encourages the behavior you actually want, not just the easy thing to measure.
vocabulary.tradingExample: Profit/loss after a trade step, but ideally adjusted for risk, drawdown, fees, and position sizing discipline.
vocabulary.agentExample: A successful tool result, user satisfaction, fewer retries, valid output, or a penalty for unsafe/irrelevant actions.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like feedback: the immediate score from the last move.

vocabulary.memoryHook: What did this lead to over time?
vocabulary.definition: The cumulative future reward the agent tries to maximize, often discounted over time.
vocabulary.intuition: Return is why RL is deeper than chasing the next reward. A small immediate loss can protect long-term upside; a pleasant immediate reward can create future damage. Return forces long-horizon thinking.
vocabulary.useCase: Whenever a decision has delayed consequences, optimize for return rather than immediate reward.
vocabulary.tradingExample: Long-term risk-adjusted portfolio growth, not whether the next trade is green for five minutes.
vocabulary.agentExample: Completing the user’s real task reliably, not merely producing a fast but shallow answer.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like compounding outcome: what the feedback adds up to.

vocabulary.definition: The agent’s behavior strategy: how it maps states to actions.
vocabulary.intuition: A policy is the agent’s habit or playbook. It can be deterministic, stochastic, hand-written, learned, simple, or complex. When behavior changes after learning, the policy is what changed.
vocabulary.useCase: Write a policy in plain English first: ‘If state looks like X, choose action Y unless risk Z is present.’ Then improve it with feedback.
vocabulary.tradingExample: If trend is up, volatility is reasonable, and risk budget is open, enter a defined-risk bullish spread; otherwise wait.
vocabulary.agentExample: If missing context is retrievable, search/read first; if ambiguity blocks action, ask; otherwise execute and verify.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like a habit: the rule that turns situations into moves.

vocabulary.definition: An estimate of expected long-term return from a state or from taking an action in a state.
vocabulary.intuition: Value is not immediate pleasure. It is future usefulness. A state can feel bad now but have high value if it opens strong future options; another can feel good now but be a trap.
vocabulary.useCase: Use value thinking to rank situations, not just actions. Ask: being here, what future does this unlock?
vocabulary.tradingExample: A pullback to support after a strong trend may have higher value than a random green candle after a stretched move.
vocabulary.agentExample: A well-structured repo with tests has high value because future changes become safer and faster.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like judgment: how promising this situation is for later.

vocabulary.memoryHook: What do I think will happen if I act?
vocabulary.definition: A representation of environment dynamics and rewards used to predict consequences of actions.
vocabulary.intuition: A model lets the agent imagine before acting. It may be explicit like a simulator, implicit like a mental model, or learned from experience. Better models make planning possible, but wrong models create confident mistakes.
vocabulary.useCase: Use model thinking when actions are expensive: simulate, forecast, or reason through likely next states before acting.
vocabulary.tradingExample: A thesis about how price may react to earnings, rates, volatility, or liquidity after entering a trade.
vocabulary.agentExample: Predicting that editing a dependency file requires running tests and may affect deployment.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like imagination: what the agent thinks will happen next.

vocabulary.memoryHook: Try to learn what I do not know.
vocabulary.definition: Choosing uncertain actions to gain information that may improve future decisions.
vocabulary.intuition: Exploration is disciplined curiosity. It is not random chaos; it is paying a learning cost to discover better actions. Without exploration, an agent can get stuck repeating the first thing that seemed okay.
vocabulary.useCase: Reserve a bounded budget for experiments. Explore enough to learn, but cap downside so learning does not destroy the system.
vocabulary.tradingExample: Paper-trading a new setup or using very small size before trusting it with real capital.
vocabulary.agentExample: Trying a new tool/workflow on a safe small task to learn whether it improves future execution.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like controlled curiosity: spend a little to learn more.

vocabulary.definition: Choosing the best-known action based on current knowledge to obtain reward.
vocabulary.intuition: Exploitation is disciplined execution. It converts learning into results. Too much exploitation becomes rigidity; too little means you never benefit from what you already know.
vocabulary.useCase: When evidence is strong and uncertainty is low, stop experimenting and execute the proven policy consistently.
vocabulary.tradingExample: Repeatedly trading a validated risk-defined setup under the conditions where it has edge.
vocabulary.agentExample: Using the known build-test-deploy workflow instead of inventing a new process every time.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like execution: use the best-known move with discipline.

vocabulary.definition: The central tension between gathering information and taking the currently best-known action.
vocabulary.intuition: This tradeoff appears whenever learning and performance happen at the same time. The agent must decide whether today is for discovery or harvesting. Mastering RL means seeing this tension everywhere.
vocabulary.useCase: Frame decisions as portfolio allocation: some budget for reliable execution, some for experiments, and explicit rules for when experiments graduate.
vocabulary.tradingExample: Allocate most capital to proven setups while reserving tiny size for testing new market patterns.
vocabulary.agentExample: Use stable workflows for production tasks while experimenting with new agents/skills in low-risk sandboxes.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like allocation: learning budget versus execution budget.

vocabulary.definition: Feedback from an action may appear many steps after the action that caused it.
vocabulary.intuition: Delayed reward is why RL needs credit assignment. Many important choices look neutral or costly at first but matter later. The hard question is: which earlier action deserves credit or blame?
vocabulary.useCase: Look for lag between action and outcome. If lag exists, immediate metrics may be misleading.
vocabulary.tradingExample: A hedge may lose money today but save the portfolio during a later volatility spike.
vocabulary.agentExample: Writing tests slows the first edit but prevents later regressions and faster future changes.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like patience: the important consequence is not immediate.

vocabulary.memoryHook: What will happen vs what should I do?
vocabulary.definition: Prediction estimates outcomes; control chooses actions to improve outcomes.
vocabulary.intuition: Supervised learning often stops at prediction. RL cares about control: using predictions to act. A model that predicts churn is useful, but an RL framing asks which intervention should be taken next.
vocabulary.useCase: Separate dashboards from decision systems. Prediction informs; control changes the world.
vocabulary.tradingExample: Predicting volatility is prediction; choosing spread width and position size is control.
vocabulary.agentExample: Predicting a test may fail is prediction; running the test and fixing the cause is control.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like two jobs: forecast the world, then decide how to change it.

vocabulary.definition: A problem where actions unfold over time and each choice affects future situations and rewards.
vocabulary.intuition: RL is for chains of consequence, not isolated answers. The key is path dependence: what you do now changes what options, information, and risks exist later.
vocabulary.useCase: Use RL thinking when the order of decisions matters: onboarding, trading campaigns, study plans, operations, robotics, habits.
vocabulary.tradingExample: Entry, adjustment, hedge, hold, exit, and review form one sequence rather than independent trades.
vocabulary.agentExample: Plan, inspect files, edit, test, debug, commit, deploy, and verify are sequential decisions with feedback.
vocabulary.vocabularyNote: Say it like a path: each choice shapes the next situation.

connections: Agent + Environment create the loop: the agent acts, the environment responds, and experience is produced.
State → Action → Reward → Next State is the smallest RL sentence; practice seeing it in every real workflow.
Reward is not the same as Return: reward is the immediate teaching signal, return is the long-term objective.
Policy uses State to choose Action; Value judges whether a State or Action is promising for future Return.
Model-based thinking predicts next states before acting; model-free learning improves directly from lived experience.
Exploration discovers better policies; exploitation converts the current best policy into results.
analogies: Trading: agent = trader/bot, environment = market, state = setup/risk context, action = trade/hold/hedge, reward = risk-adjusted P&L, return = long-term portfolio growth.
Product: agent = product team, environment = users/market, state = current funnel metrics, action = experiment, reward = activation/retention signal, return = durable customer value.
Fitness: agent = you, state = energy/schedule/recovery, action = workout/rest/nutrition, reward = immediate feeling, return = long-term health and strength.
Agentic AI: agent = assistant workflow, environment = user/tools/files/APIs, state = task context, action = tool call or answer, reward = verified progress, return = solved user outcome.

exercises.prompt: Say only the terms first, without definitions: Agent, Environment, State, Action, Reward, Return, Policy, Value Function, Model. Then point to each term in one real system.
Define Agent, Environment, State, Action, Reward, and Return in one sentence each. No textbook language; use your own systems-builder words.
For a trading bot, identify what is inside the agent, what belongs to the environment, what the state contains, and what counts as action vs reward.
Draw the loop: State → Action → Reward → Next State → Policy update. Add Return above the loop as the long-term target.
Explain the difference between Reward and Return using junk food vs health, quick P&L vs portfolio growth, and fast answer vs verified task completion.
Create one full RL mapping for your life/fitness system and one for an agentic AI coding assistant.
Explain Chapter 1 as: RL is learning a policy for sequential decisions through feedback, under uncertainty, to maximize long-term return.

title: Multi-armed Bandits
tagline: Chapter 2 vocabulary-to-concept practice for Multi-armed Bandits.
masteryQuestion: Choose between exploring a new option and exploiting the current winner.

vocabulary.definition: k-armed bandit is the Chapter Multi-armed Bandits vocabulary handle for a specific part of the RL decision-and-learning loop.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when k-armed bandit changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map k-armed bandit to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: Action value estimates long-term desirability rather than only immediate payoff.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when action value changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map action value to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: Sample average uses observed experience samples instead of full environment enumeration.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when sample average changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map sample average to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: ε-greedy is the Chapter Multi-armed Bandits vocabulary handle for a specific part of the RL decision-and-learning loop.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when ε-greedy changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map ε-greedy to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: Optimistic initial values estimates long-term desirability rather than only immediate payoff.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when optimistic initial values changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map optimistic initial values to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: Upper Confidence Bound is the Chapter Multi-armed Bandits vocabulary handle for a specific part of the RL decision-and-learning loop.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when upper confidence bound changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map upper confidence bound to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: Gradient bandit improves parameters by moving in the direction that should increase the objective.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when gradient bandit changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map gradient bandit to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: Regret is the Chapter Multi-armed Bandits vocabulary handle for a specific part of the RL decision-and-learning loop.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when regret changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map regret to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

connections: Connect k-armed bandit → Action value → practical decision loop.
Contrast immediate feedback with long-term consequence in Multi-armed Bandits.
Name the uncertainty, the update rule, and the behavior change.
analogies: Trading: use Chapter 2 ideas to decide buy/sell/hold with risk-first feedback.
Product: treat feature experiments as actions and retention/revenue as delayed return.
Personal system: convert habits into policy rules and feedback into adaptation.

exercises.prompt: Write one-line definitions for: k-armed bandit, Action value, Sample average, ε-greedy, Optimistic initial values, Upper Confidence Bound.
For each term, answer: what changes, what is observed, and what gets updated?
Draw arrows connecting k-armed bandit, Action value, reward/feedback, and long-term return.
Create one trading, one product, and one personal-growth analogy for Multi-armed Bandits.
Choose between exploring a new option and exploiting the current winner.

title: Finite Markov Decision Processes
tagline: Chapter 3 vocabulary-to-concept practice for Finite Markov Decision Processes.
masteryQuestion: Turn a messy real-world decision into states, actions, rewards, and transitions.

vocabulary.definition: A formal framework for sequential decision problems with states, actions, transitions, and rewards.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when mdp changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map mdp to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: The assumption that the current state contains enough information for predicting the next step.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when markov property changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map markov property to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when transition probability changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map transition probability to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.useCase: Map reward function to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.useCase: Map discount factor to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: Episodic task is the Chapter Finite Markov Decision Processes vocabulary handle for a specific part of the RL decision-and-learning loop.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when episodic task changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map episodic task to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: Continuing task is the Chapter Finite Markov Decision Processes vocabulary handle for a specific part of the RL decision-and-learning loop.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when continuing task changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map continuing task to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.definition: A recursive relationship that defines value as immediate reward plus discounted future value.
vocabulary.intuition: Ask: when bellman equation changes, does the agent observe differently, choose differently, learn differently, or value the future differently?
vocabulary.useCase: Map bellman equation to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

vocabulary.useCase: Map optimal value to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

title: Temporal-Difference Learning

masteryQuestion: Update your estimate immediately after seeing the next step.

vocabulary.definition: TD learning is the Chapter Temporal-Difference Learning vocabulary handle for a specific part of the RL decision-and-learning loop.

vocabulary.useCase: Map td learning to a concrete loop: trader decision, product experiment, habit rule, or autonomous agent workflow.

title: Neuroscience
masteryQuestion: Connect algorithmic prediction error to biological learning signals.

vocabulary.definition: A biological learning signal often linked to reward prediction error.

vocabulary.definition: The difference between expected and received reward, used as a learning signal.
